ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: 'Phoenicia' is a Greek exonym (Φοινίκη); the people likely did not use a single collective name, identifying instead by city (Ṣūr/Tyre, Ṣīdūn/Sidon, Gubal/Byblos). The Phoenician script is reconstructed from limited epigraphic evidence. ETHICS-003: Phoenicia was never a unified state but a loose confederation of independent city-states sharing language and culture. Representing it as a single entity is a scholarly convenience that risks obscuring internal diversity. Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (-1000, strategy=capital_in_polygon, precision=1). CC BY 4.0.

name_variants.name: Phoenicia
name_variants.context: English scholarly name derived from Greek Φοινίκη
name_variants.source: Markoe, G. Phoenicians (2000)

name_variants.name: Φοινίκη
name_variants.context: Ancient Greek name, possibly meaning 'land of purple dye'
name_variants.source: Herodotus, Histories VII.89

name_variants.name: Fenicia
name_variants.context: Italian scholarly name
name_variants.source: Moscati, S. I Fenici (1988)

name_variants.name: Canaan
name_variants.context: Bronze Age designation for the broader region including Phoenician cities
name_variants.source: Aubet, M.E. The Phoenicians and the West (2001)

territory_changes.description: Phoenician city-states emerge as independent powers following the Bronze Age collapse and withdrawal of Egyptian and Hittite authority. Evidence is primarily archaeological — destructions at many Levantine sites circa -1200 to -1150, with Phoenician cities showing continuity.

Traditional date for founding of Carthage by Tyrian colonists. Archaeological evidence supports settlement from late 9th century BCE. Represents Phoenician colonial expansion across the Mediterranean.
Incorporation into the Achaemenid Empire under Cyrus the Great. Phoenician cities retained significant autonomy as vassal states providing naval power to Persia.

sources.citation: Markoe, G. Phoenicians, Peoples of the Past (2000)
Aubet, M.E. The Phoenicians and the West: Politics, Colonies and Trade, 2nd ed. (2001)
Moscati, S. (ed.) I Fenici (1988)
Herodotus, Histories

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: Name in Hebrew as used in the Tanakh. ETHICS-003: CRITICAL — the historicity and extent of the United Monarchy (under Saul, David, Solomon) is one of the most intensely debated topics in Near Eastern archaeology. Biblical minimalists (Finkelstein, Silberman) argue the United Monarchy was a small chieftaincy later mythologized; maximalists (Mazar, Kitchen) argue for a larger polity. Archaeological evidence (Tel Dan stele, Mesha stele) confirms a 'House of David' and a polity called Israel by the 9th century BCE. The early dates (-1047 to -930) carry very low confidence. This entity should NOT be used to support any modern territorial claims. Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (-1000, strategy=capital_in_polygon, precision=1). CC BY 4.0.

name_variants.name: Kingdom of Israel
name_variants.context: English name covering both united and northern kingdom periods
name_variants.source: Finkelstein, I. & Silberman, N.A. The Bible Unearthed (2001)

name_variants.name: Northern Kingdom
name_variants.context: English name for Israel after the split from Judah
name_variants.source: Liverani, M. Israel's History and the History of Israel (2005)

name_variants.name: Bīt-Ḫumrī
name_variants.context: Assyrian name meaning 'House of Omri', used in Neo-Assyrian inscriptions
name_variants.source: ANET, Shalmaneser III Black Obelisk inscription

name_variants.name: Regno di Israele
name_variants.context: Italian scholarly name
name_variants.source: Liverani, M. Oltre la Bibbia (2003)

territory_changes.description: Traditional date for establishment of a unified Israelite monarchy under David. Archaeological evidence for a centralized state at this date is limited and disputed. Settlement patterns in the hill country show gradual urbanization rather than sudden state formation. Confidence is very low for the united monarchy period.
Traditional split of the United Monarchy into Israel (north) and Judah (south). The northern kingdom, with its capital eventually at Samaria, became the more prosperous and powerful of the two.

Destruction by the Neo-Assyrian Empire under Sargon II (completing Shalmaneser V's siege). Mass deportation of the population — Assyrian records claim 27,290 people deported. The 'Ten Lost Tribes' tradition reflects this forced displacement. This is well-attested in both Assyrian and biblical sources.

sources.citation: Finkelstein, I. & Silberman, N.A. The Bible Unearthed: Archaeology's New Vision of Ancient Israel (2001)
Liverani, M. Israel's History and the History of Israel (2005)
Mazar, A. Archaeology of the Land of the Bible: 10,000–586 B.C.E. (1990)
Tel Dan Stele (9th century BCE)
Sargon II, Annals (Khorsabad inscription)

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: Name in Hebrew as used in the Tanakh. ETHICS-003: Better attested than the United Monarchy period, with archaeological evidence from the 8th century BCE onward (Hezekiah's tunnel, LMLK seals, Lachish letters). Earlier periods carry lower confidence. The destruction of Jerusalem in -586 and the Babylonian Exile are well-documented in both Babylonian and biblical sources. As with the Kingdom of Israel, this entity should NOT be used to support modern territorial claims. Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (-700, strategy=capital_in_polygon, precision=1). CC BY 4.0.

name_variants.name: Kingdom of Judah
name_variants.context: English name for the southern kingdom
name_variants.source: Finkelstein, I. & Silberman, N.A. The Bible Unearthed (2001)

name_variants.name: Yāhūdu
name_variants.context: Akkadian/Babylonian name found in cuneiform texts
name_variants.source: Pearce, L.E. & Wunsch, C. Documents of Judean Exiles and West Semites in Babylonia (2014)

name_variants.name: Regno di Giuda
name_variants.context: Italian scholarly name
name_variants.source: Liverani, M. Oltre la Bibbia (2003)

territory_changes.description: Traditional date for the division of the United Monarchy. Judah centered on Jerusalem and the southern hill country. Archaeological evidence suggests Judah was a small, relatively poor polity until the 8th century BCE.

Sennacherib's campaign devastated Judah, destroying 46 fortified cities (Lachish reliefs). Jerusalem survived, possibly through tribute payment. This event is exceptionally well-documented in Assyrian, biblical, and archaeological sources.

Destruction of Jerusalem and the Temple by Nebuchadnezzar II of Babylon. Mass deportation to Babylonia (the Babylonian Exile). Babylonian Chronicles and Lachish letters provide contemporary documentation. One of the most consequential events in the religious history of the ancient world.

sources.citation: Finkelstein, I. & Silberman, N.A. The Bible Unearthed (2001)
Lachish Letters (Lachish III, Tufnell 1953)
Babylonian Chronicles (British Museum tablets)
Pearce, L.E. & Wunsch, C. Documents of Judean Exiles and West Semites in Babylonia in the Collection of David Sofer (2014)

ethical_notes: ETHICS-003: The identification of the archaeological site at Hisarlik with Homer's Troy is widely accepted but not universally agreed upon. The site shows multiple destruction layers. Troy VIIa (c. -1180) is the best candidate for a 'Trojan War' destruction but the historicity of the Homeric war itself remains debated. The Hittite texts mention 'Wilusa' which may correspond to Ilion/Troy. Separating archaeological evidence from literary tradition is essential. Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (-2000, strategy=capital_in_polygon, precision=1). CC BY 4.0.

name_variants.name: Troy
name_variants.context: English name derived from Latin Troia
name_variants.source: Bryce, T. The Trojans and Their Neighbours (2006)

name_variants.name: Ἴλιον
name_variants.context: Alternative ancient Greek name, as in Homer's Iliad
name_variants.source: Homer, Iliad

name_variants.name: Wilusa
name_variants.context: Hittite name from treaty texts, possibly corresponding to Ilion
name_variants.source: Hawkins, J.D. Tarkasnawa King of Mira (1998)

name_variants.name: Troia
name_variants.context: Latin name used by Virgil and Roman authors
name_variants.source: Virgil, Aeneid

territory_changes.description: Troy II shows evidence of a wealthy fortified citadel with monumental architecture. Schliemann's 'Treasure of Priam' actually dates to this period, not the Homeric era. The site controlled the strategic entrance to the Dardanelles.

Destruction layer at Troy VIIa, the leading candidate for the 'Trojan War' if it occurred. Evidence shows fire and violence, but the attackers' identity is unknown. Whether Mycenaean Greeks were responsible, as Homer narrates, cannot be confirmed archaeologically.

sources.citation: Korfmann, M. Troia/Wilusa: General Background and a Digest of Excavation Results (1988-2003)
Bryce, T. The Trojans and Their Neighbours (2006)
Homer, Iliad (c. 8th century BCE)
Hawkins, J.D. Tarkasnawa King of Mira, Anatolian Studies 48 (1998)

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: The name 'Minoan' is a modern scholarly invention by Arthur Evans (1900), derived from the mythical King Minos. We do not know what the Minoans called themselves. Linear A remains undeciphered, so the original language and self-designation are unknown. ETHICS-003: The political organization of Minoan Crete is debated — it may have been a single palace-centered state, a confederation of palace-states, or something without modern parallels. Classification as 'kingdom' is a scholarly convenience. Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (-2000, strategy=capital_in_polygon, precision=1). CC BY 4.0.

name_variants.name: Minoan Crete
name_variants.context: Modern English scholarly name coined by Arthur Evans
name_variants.source: Evans, A. The Palace of Minos at Knossos (1921-1935)

name_variants.name: Keftiu
name_variants.context: Egyptian name for Crete, found in New Kingdom texts and tomb paintings
name_variants.source: Wachsmann, S. Aegeans in the Theban Tombs (1987)

name_variants.name: Creta minoica
name_variants.context: Italian scholarly name
name_variants.source: Preziosi, D. & Hitchcock, L.A. Aegean Art and Architecture (1999)

territory_changes.description: Construction of the first palaces (Knossos, Phaistos, Malia, Zakros) marking the Protopalatial period. Whether this represents political unification of Crete under Knossos or independent palatial polities is debated. Evidence is entirely archaeological — no readable texts from this period.

Destruction and reconstruction of all major palaces marks the transition to the Neopalatial period. Cause unknown — earthquake, internal conflict, or external invasion are all proposed. Knossos emerges as clearly dominant afterward.

All palaces except Knossos are destroyed. Knossos continues under Mycenaean Greek control (Linear B tablets appear). Whether this represents a Mycenaean invasion or an internal takeover is debated. The Minoan civilization as an independent entity effectively ends.

sources.citation: Evans, A. The Palace of Minos at Knossos, 4 vols. (1921-1935)
Preziosi, D. & Hitchcock, L.A. Aegean Art and Architecture (1999)
Driessen, J. & Macdonald, C. The Troubled Island: Minoan Crete before and after the Santorini Eruption (1997)

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: The Greek name Μυκῆναι is attested in Linear B as mu-ka-na. Unlike the Minoans, the Mycenaeans can be identified as Greek speakers through deciphered Linear B tablets. ETHICS-003: 'Mycenaean Greece' is a modern construct — the Mycenaean world consisted of multiple independent palace-states (Mycenae, Pylos, Tiryns, Thebes, etc.). Whether Mycenae held any primacy (as Homer suggests with Agamemnon) is debated. Representing it as a single kingdom oversimplifies the political landscape. Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (-1500, strategy=capital_in_polygon, precision=1). CC BY 4.0.

name_variants.name: Mycenaean Greece
name_variants.context: Modern scholarly name for Late Bronze Age Greek civilization
name_variants.source: Chadwick, J. The Mycenaean World (1976)

name_variants.name: Ahhiyawa
name_variants.context: Hittite name possibly referring to Mycenaean Greeks, debated identification with Achaeans
name_variants.source: Beckman, G., Bryce, T. & Cline, E. The Ahhiyawa Texts (2011)

name_variants.name: Grecia micenea
name_variants.context: Italian scholarly name
name_variants.source: Dickinson, O. The Aegean Bronze Age (1994)

territory_changes.description: Shaft Graves at Mycenae mark the emergence of a wealthy warrior elite. Rich gold grave goods (including the 'Mask of Agamemnon', actually centuries earlier than the legendary king) demonstrate connections with Minoan Crete and the wider Mediterranean.
Mycenaean Greeks take control of Knossos. Linear B (Greek) replaces Linear A at Knossos. This represents the most significant Mycenaean territorial expansion we can document.

Collapse of all Mycenaean palace centers over approximately a century. Causes debated — invasion (Sea Peoples, Dorians), systems collapse, drought, earthquake, or combination. Linear B writing disappears. Greece enters the so-called 'Dark Ages'. Population decline estimated at 75-90% in some regions.

sources.citation: Chadwick, J. The Mycenaean World (1976)
Beckman, G., Bryce, T. & Cline, E. The Ahhiyawa Texts (2011)
Cline, E.H. 1177 B.C.: The Year Civilization Collapsed (2014)
Linear B tablets from Pylos and Knossos

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: 'Scythia' (Σκυθία) is a Greek exonym. The Scythians were Iranian-speaking nomads who left no written records of their own. Our knowledge comes almost entirely from Greek (Herodotus) and Achaemenid sources, supplemented by spectacular kurgan archaeology. ETHICS-003: The Scythians had no fixed capital or borders in the conventional sense. Kamenskoe is the largest known Scythian settlement but 'capital' is misleading for a nomadic confederation. The boundaries of 'Scythia' shifted constantly.

name_variants.name: Scythia
name_variants.context: English name from Greek Σκυθία
name_variants.source: Herodotus, Histories IV

name_variants.name: Sakā
name_variants.context: Old Persian name used in Achaemenid inscriptions (Behistun)
name_variants.source: Darius I, Behistun inscription

name_variants.name: Iškuzaya
name_variants.context: Assyrian name for Scythians encountered during their incursion into the Near East
name_variants.source: Esarhaddon's treaty texts, ANET

name_variants.name: Scizia
name_variants.context: Italian scholarly name
name_variants.source: Melyukova, A.I. in Cambridge Ancient History (1982)

territory_changes.description: Scythians displace the Cimmerians from the Pontic-Caspian steppe, as reported by Herodotus. Archaeological evidence (weapon types, horse gear) supports a major cultural change in the region. The Scythians may have originated further east in Central Asia.

Darius I of Persia invades Scythia and is forced to retreat. Herodotus provides a detailed (if embellished) account. This event demonstrated the difficulty of conquering nomadic peoples and is one of the few Scythian 'events' with textual corroboration.
Sarmatian tribes gradually displace the Scythians from the steppe. The process was probably gradual rather than a single event. Remnant Scythian populations persisted in Crimea into the Roman period.

sources.citation: Herodotus, Histories IV (c. 440 BCE)
Rolle, R. The World of the Scythians (1989)
Cunliffe, B. The Scythians: Nomad Warriors of the Steppe (2019)
Darius I, Behistun inscription

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: 'Sarmatia' is a Greek/Roman exonym. The Sarmatians were Iranian-speaking nomadic peoples (Roxolani, Iazyges, Alans, Aorsi) who left no written records. ETHICS-003: Even more than the Scythians, the Sarmatians were a loose grouping of distinct tribes rather than a political entity. There was no 'Sarmatian state'. The capital_lat/lon represent a rough center of the Pontic-Caspian steppe, not any actual settlement. Representing Sarmatia as a single entity is a significant scholarly simplification.

name_variants.name: Sarmatia
name_variants.context: English name from Greek/Latin Sarmatia
name_variants.source: Ptolemy, Geography III.5

name_variants.name: Sauromatae
name_variants.context: Earlier Greek name used by Herodotus
name_variants.source: Herodotus, Histories IV.21

name_variants.name: Sarmazia
name_variants.context: Italian scholarly name
name_variants.source: Sulimirski, T. The Sarmatians (1970)

territory_changes.description: Sarmatian tribes progressively displace Scythians from the western steppe. Archaeological evidence shows replacement of Scythian-type burials with Sarmatian ones (characterized by different weapon types, mirror types, and tamga symbols). The process was gradual, not a single event.

Hunnic invasion destroys Sarmatian dominance on the steppe. The Alans (a Sarmatian subgroup) are scattered — some join the Huns, others flee westward (reaching as far as North Africa with the Vandals). Ammianus Marcellinus provides a near-contemporary account.

sources.citation: Sulimirski, T. The Sarmatians, Ancient Peoples and Places (1970)
Herodotus, Histories IV.21 (c. 440 BCE)
Ammianus Marcellinus, Res Gestae XXXI (c. 390 CE)
Ptolemy, Geography III.5 (c. 150 CE)

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: Neither 'Harappa' nor 'Indus Valley' are original names — both are modern designations. The Indus script remains undeciphered, so the civilization's self-designation is unknown. Using Sanskrit is anachronistic, as Sanskrit postdates this civilization, but is the closest available ISO 639 code for the region. ETHICS-003: The political organization is entirely unknown. Whether there was a single state, multiple city-states, or some other form of organization cannot be determined without deciphering the script. Calling it a 'city-state' is uncertain — 'civilization' would be more accurate but is not in our entity_type schema. ETHICS-004: The relationship between the Indus Valley Civilization and later Hindu civilization is politically charged in modern India and should be treated with scholarly caution. Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (-2000, strategy=exact_name, precision=1). CC BY 4.0.

name_variants.name: Indus Valley Civilization
name_variants.context: Most common English name, though the civilization extended well beyond the Indus valley
name_variants.source: Possehl, G.L. The Indus Civilization: A Contemporary Perspective (2002)

name_variants.name: Harappan Civilization
name_variants.context: Alternative English name from the first excavated site
name_variants.source: Kenoyer, J.M. Ancient Cities of the Indus Valley Civilization (1998)

name_variants.name: Civiltà della valle dell'Indo
name_variants.context: Italian scholarly name
name_variants.source: Tosi, M. in Civiltà antiche del Medio Oriente (1996)

name_variants.name: Sarasvati-Sindhu Civilization
name_variants.context: Alternative name emphasizing the Ghaggar-Hakra/Sarasvati river sites; used in some Indian scholarship, politically motivated expansion of the name
name_variants.source: Lal, B.B. The Sarasvati Flows On (2002)

territory_changes.description: Mature Harappan phase begins, with standardized weights, seals, urban planning, and writing. The transition from Early to Mature Harappan appears gradual rather than a conquest event. Cities like Mohenjo-daro and Harappa show remarkable urban sophistication without clear evidence of a ruling class or military.

Gradual de-urbanization and decline. Mohenjo-daro abandoned. Causes debated: river course changes (especially the drying of the Ghaggar-Hakra), climate change, overexploitation of resources. There is NO evidence for the outdated 'Aryan invasion' theory as a cause of the decline. The process was gradual, not catastrophic.

sources.citation: Possehl, G.L. The Indus Civilization: A Contemporary Perspective (2002)
Kenoyer, J.M. Ancient Cities of the Indus Valley Civilization (1998)
Marshall, J. Mohenjo-daro and the Indus Civilization (1931)
Wright, R.P. The Ancient Indus: Urbanism, Economy, and Society (2010)

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: Greek administrative language; the population was originally Thracian (Thyni/Bithyni tribes). ETHICS-003: Relatively well-attested through Greek and Roman literary sources and coinage, though archaeological evidence for the pre-Hellenistic period is limited. Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (-200, strategy=capital_in_polygon, precision=1). CC BY 4.0.

name_variants.name: Kingdom of Bithynia
name_variants.context: English name for the Hellenistic kingdom in northwestern Anatolia
name_variants.source: Magie, D. Roman Rule in Asia Minor (1950)

name_variants.name: Bithynia
name_variants.context: Latin name used by Roman sources
name_variants.source: Pliny the Younger, Letters to Trajan

name_variants.name: Bitinia
name_variants.context: Italian scholarly name
name_variants.source: Fernoux, H.-L. Notables et élites des cités de Bithynie (2004)

territory_changes.description: Zipoetes I declares independence from the Diadochi successor states following the Battle of Ipsus. The Bithynian dynasty descended from Thracian tribal leaders who had maintained autonomy during the Achaemenid period.
Nicomedes I founds Nicomedia as the new capital, invites Galatian Celts into Anatolia as mercenary allies — a decision that profoundly transformed the region.
Nicomedes IV bequeaths the kingdom to the Roman Republic in his will, a practice also seen with Pergamon. This triggered the Third Mithridatic War as Pontus contested Roman control.

sources.citation: Magie, D. Roman Rule in Asia Minor, 2 vols. (1950)
Fernoux, H.-L. Notables et élites des cités de Bithynie aux époques hellénistique et romaine (2004)
Memnon of Heraclea, History of Heraclea (fragments in Photius)

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: Greek name as used in Hellenistic administrative documents. ETHICS-003: Well-attested through extensive archaeological excavation (German excavations from 1878), inscriptions, and literary sources. The Pergamon Altar (now in Berlin) raises ethical questions about archaeological heritage and colonial-era artifact removal — the altar was transported to Germany in the 19th century. Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (-200, strategy=fuzzy_name, precision=1). CC BY 4.0.

name_variants.name: Kingdom of Pergamon
name_variants.context: English name for the Attalid kingdom
name_variants.source: Hansen, E.V. The Attalids of Pergamon (1971)

name_variants.name: Attalid Kingdom
name_variants.context: Alternative English name from the ruling dynasty
name_variants.source: Allen, R.E. The Attalid Kingdom (1983)

name_variants.name: Pergamo
name_variants.context: Italian scholarly name
name_variants.source: Bohn, R. Altertümer von Pergamon (1885)

territory_changes.description: Philetaerus, originally a Seleucid officer, seizes Pergamon and its treasury. Establishes an effectively independent dynasty, though nominally Seleucid vassal. The founding was an act of political opportunism during the Diadochi wars.

Attalus I defeats the Galatian Celts near Sardis, adopts the title Soter ('Saviour'), and formally declares royal status. The famous 'Dying Gaul' statue commemorates this victory — note that it depicts the defeated enemy with dignity, unusual in ancient art.

Attalus III bequeaths the kingdom to the Roman Republic in his will. The bequest triggered Aristonicus's revolt (Eumenes III) supported by enslaved people and the poor, brutally suppressed by Rome. The territory became the province of Asia.

sources.citation: Hansen, E.V. The Attalids of Pergamon, 2nd ed. (1971)
Allen, R.E. The Attalid Kingdom: A Constitutional History (1983)
Strabo, Geography XIII.4 (c. 20 CE)
Bohn, R. Altertümer von Pergamon (1885-)

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: Greek name as used in inscriptions and literary sources. The kingdom controlled both sides of the Cimmerian Bosporus (Kerch Strait). ETHICS-003: Well-attested through Greek inscriptions, coinage, and archaeological excavations in Crimea and Taman Peninsula. The population was mixed Greek, Sindian, Maeotian, and Scythian — a genuinely multicultural polity. The Bosporan Kingdom is of particular interest for understanding Greek-non-Greek interaction in the ancient world. Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (-100, strategy=exact_name, precision=1). CC BY 4.0.

name_variants.name: Bosporan Kingdom
name_variants.context: English name for the kingdom on the Cimmerian Bosporus
name_variants.source: Braund, D. (ed.) Scythians and Greeks (2005)

name_variants.name: Regnum Bosporanum
name_variants.context: Latin name used in Roman-period sources
name_variants.source: Strabo, Geography VII.4; XI.2

name_variants.name: Regno del Bosforo
name_variants.context: Italian scholarly name
name_variants.source: Rostovtzeff, M.I. Iranians and Greeks in South Russia (1922)

territory_changes.description: The Archaeanactid dynasty unifies Greek colonies around the Cimmerian Bosporus. The founding date is approximate, based on later literary tradition.

Spartocid dynasty takes power, possibly of Thracian or Sindian origin. Under the Spartocids the kingdom expands to include non-Greek peoples (Sindians, Maeotians) and becomes a major grain exporter to Athens.
Final collapse under Hunnic pressure. The kingdom had already been a Roman client state for centuries and had survived Gothic raids in the 3rd century. End date is approximate.

sources.citation: Braund, D. (ed.) Scythians and Greeks: Cultural Interactions in Scythia, Athens and the Early Roman Empire (2005)
Rostovtzeff, M.I. Iranians and Greeks in South Russia (1922)
Strabo, Geography VII.4, XI.2 (c. 20 CE)
Gajdukevič, V.F. Das Bosporanische Reich (1971)

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: 'Galatia' is the Greek name for the Celtic tribes who settled in central Anatolia. They spoke Galatian, a Celtic language. ETHICS-003: The Galatians present a fascinating case of Celtic peoples far from their Western European homeland. Their political organization was tribal (three tribes: Tolistobogii, Tectosages, Trocmi), each with their own territory. Calling Galatia a 'kingdom' is an approximation — it was more of a tribal confederation that occasionally had strong leaders (tetrarchs, later kings). Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (-200, strategy=capital_in_polygon, precision=1). CC BY 4.0.

name_variants.name: Galatia
name_variants.context: English name for the Celtic territory in Anatolia
name_variants.source: Mitchell, S. Anatolia: Land, Men, and Gods in Asia Minor (1993)

name_variants.name: Gallogrecia
name_variants.context: Alternative Latin name emphasizing Celtic (Gallic) identity
name_variants.source: Livy, Ab Urbe Condita XXXVIII

name_variants.name: Galazia
name_variants.context: Italian scholarly name
name_variants.source: Mitchell, S. Anatolia (1993)

territory_changes.description: Celtic tribes (20,000 warriors with families, per Livy) cross into Asia Minor, invited by Nicomedes I of Bithynia as mercenaries. They quickly established dominance over the interior, raiding Greek coastal cities for decades. This was part of the broader Celtic migrations that affected the entire Mediterranean.

Roman consul Cn. Manlius Vulso campaigns against the Galatians, defeating them at Mount Olympus and Mount Magaba. Livy describes the campaign in detail. The Galatians were subordinated but not destroyed.

Death of the last Galatian king Amyntas; Augustus annexes Galatia as a Roman province. Galatian Celtic language survived for centuries — Jerome (4th century CE) noted its similarity to the Treveri dialect in Gaul.

sources.citation: Mitchell, S. Anatolia: Land, Men, and Gods in Asia Minor, 2 vols. (1993)
Livy, Ab Urbe Condita XXXVIII (c. 20 BCE)
Strobel, K. Die Galater: Geschichte und Eigenart der keltischen Staatenbildung (1996)
Jerome, Commentary on Galatians, preface (c. 386 CE)

ethical_notes:
ETHICS-001: Greek administrative name; the population was mixed Armenian, Syriac, and Greek. The royal dynasty claimed dual descent from Alexander the Great and the Achaemenid dynasty — a conscious syncretic identity. ETHICS-003: Commagene is exceptionally well-known for the monumental sanctuary at Nemrud Dagi (Mount Nemrut), a UNESCO World Heritage Site, built by Antiochus I. The syncretism of Greek and Persian religion visible there is historically significant. Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (-1, strategy=capital_in_polygon, precision=1). CC BY 4.0.

[v6.7.2] boundary precedente era un mismatch geografico (polygon >10x l'estensione storica attesa). Sostituito con name_seeded_boundary ancorato alla capital. Vedi ETHICS-006.

name_variants.name: Commagene
name_variants.context: English name from Greek Κομμαγηνή
name_variants.source: Wagner, J. Seleukeia am Euphrat/Zeugma (1976)

name_variants.name: Kummuḫu
name_variants.context: Assyrian name for the earlier Neo-Hittite kingdom in the same region
name_variants.source: Hawkins, J.D. Corpus of Hieroglyphic Luwian Inscriptions (2000)

name_variants.name: Commagene
name_variants.context: Italian scholarly name (same as English)
name_variants.source: Sullivan, R.D. Near Eastern Royalty and Rome (1990)

territory_changes.description: Ptolemaeus, a Seleucid governor, declares independence as the Seleucid Empire fragments. The region had been the Neo-Hittite kingdom of Kummuḫu before Assyrian conquest.
Antiochus I secures Roman recognition by supporting Pompey against the Seleucids. This inaugurated Commagene's precarious existence as a buffer state between Rome and Parthia.

Vespasian annexes Commagene as part of the Roman province of Syria. The last king, Antiochus IV, was deposed and exiled to Rome where he lived comfortably. The Commagenian dynasty is last attested in the 3rd century CE in Rome.

sources.citation: Sullivan, R.D. Near Eastern Royalty and Rome, 100-30 B.C. (1990)
Wagner, J. Seleukeia am Euphrat/Zeugma (1976)
Josephus, Jewish War VII.7 (c. 75 CE)
Nemrud Dagi inscriptions (Antiochus I Nomos inscription)

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: 'Dacia' is the Latin/Greek exonym. The Dacians' own name for their land is unknown (they left no written records). The Dacian language is almost entirely unattested beyond a few glosses and place names. ETHICS-002: The Roman conquest under Trajan (101-106 CE) was one of the most thoroughly documented ancient conquests — Trajan's Column in Rome provides a visual narrative, though it represents the Roman perspective exclusively. The Dacians were portrayed both as worthy adversaries and as barbarians. Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (-1, strategy=exact_name, precision=1). CC BY 4.0.

name_variants.name: Dacia
name_variants.context: English name from Latin Dacia
name_variants.source: Oltean, I.A. Dacia: Landscape, Colonisation and Romanisation (2007)

name_variants.name: Γετία
name_variants.context: Greek name; Greeks called the same people Getae
name_variants.source: Herodotus, Histories IV.93

name_variants.name: Dacia
name_variants.context: Italian scholarly name (same as English)
name_variants.source: Coarelli, F. La Colonna Traiana (1999)

territory_changes.description: Burebista unifies the Dacian tribes, creating a powerful kingdom. Strabo reports he also conquered Celtic Boii and Taurisci. His state fragmented after his assassination (c. -44). The date -82 is Strabo's approximate date for the start of Burebista's consolidation.
Decebalus reunifies the Dacian kingdom and defeats the Roman general Cornelius Fuscus, capturing legionary standards — a major Roman humiliation. Domitian was forced to conclude a subsidized peace.

Trajan conquers Dacia in two campaigns (101-102, 105-106). Sarmizegetusa Regia destroyed. Massive deportation and colonization followed. Dio Cassius mentions 500,000 prisoners. Trajan's Column and the Forum of Trajan in Rome were built with Dacian spoils. The conquest was devastating for the indigenous population.

sources.citation: Oltean, I.A. Dacia: Landscape, Colonisation and Romanisation (2007)
Coarelli, F. La Colonna Traiana (1999)
Dio Cassius, Roman History LXVIII (c. 220 CE)
Strabo, Geography VII.3 (c. 20 CE)

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: Greek name as used in ancient sources. Epirus was considered semi-barbarian by the Athenians despite being Greek-speaking (Northwest Greek dialect). The ruling Molossian dynasty claimed descent from Achilles via Neoptolemus — a claim that legitimized Pyrrhus's later campaigns. ETHICS-003: The political structure evolved from tribal confederation (Molossians, Chaonians, Thesprotians) to monarchy to federal republic (Epirote League, after -232).

name_variants.name: Kingdom of Epirus
name_variants.context: English name for the monarchical period
name_variants.source: Hammond, N.G.L. Epirus (1967)

name_variants.name: Epirote League
name_variants.context: English name for the republican period after the end of the monarchy
name_variants.source: Cabanes, P. L'Épire de la mort de Pyrrhos à la conquête romaine (1976)

name_variants.name: Epiro
name_variants.context: Italian scholarly name
name_variants.source: Hammond, N.G.L. Epirus (1967)

territory_changes.description: Consolidation of the Molossian royal house over the Epirote tribes. Tharrhypas traditionally credited with introducing Greek-style governance. The date is approximate; the political history before Pyrrhus is poorly documented.

Pyrrhus invades Italy at the invitation of Tarentum, defeating the Romans at Heraclea and Asculum but at devastating cost — the origin of the phrase 'Pyrrhic victory'. His campaigns in Italy and Sicily (-280 to -275) are detailed by Plutarch.

Roman general Aemilius Paullus devastates Epirus after the Third Macedonian War. According to Livy, 150,000 Epirotes were enslaved from 70 cities in a single day. This was one of the most brutal Roman mass-enslavements on record and effectively destroyed Epirote civilization.

sources.citation: Hammond, N.G.L. Epirus: The Geography, the Ancient Remains, the History and Topography of Epirus and Adjacent Areas (1967)
Cabanes, P. L'Épire de la mort de Pyrrhos à la conquête romaine (1976)
Plutarch, Life of Pyrrhus (c. 100 CE)
Livy, Ab Urbe Condita XLV.34 (c. 20 BCE)

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: Greek name as used in ancient texts and inscriptions. ETHICS-002: The destruction of Corinth by Rome in -146 BCE (the same year as Carthage) was an act of exemplary violence that eliminated one of the wealthiest and most culturally significant Greek cities. Roman sources (Polybius) provide the primary account. ETHICS-003: Corinth controlled the strategically vital isthmus and two harbors (Lechaeum on the Corinthian Gulf, Cenchreae on the Saronic Gulf). Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (-500, strategy=capital_in_polygon, precision=1). CC BY 4.0.

name_variants.name: Corinth
name_variants.context: English name from Latin Corinthus
name_variants.source: Salmon, J.B. Wealthy Corinth (1984)

name_variants.name: Corinthus
name_variants.context: Latin name
name_variants.source: Cicero, De Lege Agraria II.87

name_variants.name: Corinto
name_variants.context: Italian scholarly name
name_variants.source: Salmon, J.B. Wealthy Corinth (1984)

territory_changes.description: Corinth founds Syracuse, which would become one of the most powerful Greek cities. Corinthian colonization was extensive — also Corcyra (Corfu), Potidaea, and others. These colonies became independent but maintained cultural and sometimes political ties.

Cypselus overthrows the Bacchiad oligarchy and establishes a tyranny. Under the Cypselid tyrants (Cypselus and Periander), Corinth became one of the wealthiest and most artistically productive Greek cities.

Roman consul Lucius Mummius destroys Corinth completely. The male population was killed, women and children enslaved, artworks looted. The city lay desolate for a century until Caesar refounded it as Colonia Laus Iulia Corinthiensis in -44. Polybius, an eyewitness, describes the destruction.

sources.citation: Salmon, J.B. Wealthy Corinth: A History of the City to 338 BC (1984)
Polybius, Histories XXXIX (c. 150 BCE)
Williams, C.K. & Bookidis, N. (eds.) Corinth XX: The Centenary (2003)
Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War I.13 (c. 400 BCE)

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: Greek name as used in ancient sources. ETHICS-002: Syracuse's history includes some of the most dramatic episodes of ancient warfare — the Athenian disaster of -413 (one of the greatest military catastrophes in Greek history), and the Roman siege of -214 to -212 during which Archimedes was killed. The treatment of defeated Athenian prisoners in the stone quarries was notoriously brutal. ETHICS-003: Syracuse at its peak was arguably the most powerful Greek city, rivaling Athens in population and cultural production. Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (-500, strategy=capital_in_polygon, precision=1). CC BY 4.0.

name_variants.name: Syracuse
name_variants.context: English name from Latin Syracusae
name_variants.source: Wilson, R.J.A. Sicily under the Roman Empire (1990)

name_variants.name: Syracusae
name_variants.context: Latin name
name_variants.source: Cicero, In Verrem II.4

name_variants.name: Siracusa
name_variants.context: Italian name (modern city preserves the name)
name_variants.source: De Angelis, F. Archaic and Classical Greek Sicily (2016)

territory_changes.description: Founded by Corinthian colonists led by Archias. The colonization involved displacement of the indigenous Siculi population. Syracuse rapidly grew due to its excellent harbor and fertile hinterland.

Syracuse defeats the massive Athenian expedition — the greatest military disaster in Athenian history. Thucydides provides a magisterial account. Approximately 40,000 Athenians and allies were killed or enslaved. Prisoners were confined in stone quarries under horrific conditions.

Roman general Marcellus captures Syracuse after a two-year siege. Archimedes was killed during the sack despite orders to spare him. The city was thoroughly looted — Marcellus stripped it of artworks. Livy and Plutarch describe the fall in detail. This marked the end of Syracuse as an independent power.

sources.citation: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War VI-VII (c. 400 BCE)
De Angelis, F. Archaic and Classical Greek Sicily: A Social and Economic History (2016)
Plutarch, Life of Marcellus (c. 100 CE)
Wilson, R.J.A. Sicily under the Roman Empire (1990)

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: 'Colchis' (Κολχίς) is the Greek exonym — the native Colchian name is unknown. The Colchians are identified with the Kartvelian (Georgian) peoples. Colchis is famous in Greek mythology as the destination of the Argonauts and the homeland of Medea. ETHICS-003: Separating mythological from historical Colchis is essential. Archaeological evidence (Colchian bronze culture) supports a distinct civilization, but its political organization is poorly understood. Urartian texts mention Qulḫa, providing independent confirmation of its existence. Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (-700, strategy=capital_in_polygon, precision=1). CC BY 4.0.

name_variants.name: Colchis
name_variants.context: English name from Greek Κολχίς
name_variants.source: Braund, D. Georgia in Antiquity (1994)

name_variants.name: Qulḫa
name_variants.context: Urartian name from royal inscriptions
name_variants.source: Melikishvili, G.A. Urartskie klinoobraznye nadpisi (1960)

name_variants.name: Colchide
name_variants.context: Italian scholarly name
name_variants.source: Braund, D. Georgia in Antiquity (1994)

name_variants.name: კოლხეთი
name_variants.context: Georgian name, modern scholarly usage
name_variants.source: Lordkipanidze, O. Phasis: The River and the City in Colchis (2000)

territory_changes.description: Colchian bronze culture flourishes. Urartian king Sarduri II mentions campaigning against Qulḫa, indicating a significant polity. Archaeological evidence includes distinctive Colchian axes and rich burial goods. The political structure may have been a loose confederation of tribes.

Colchis likely came under Achaemenid influence as a tribute-paying region. Herodotus (III.97) mentions Colchians sending gifts to the Persian king, including 100 boys and 100 girls — a detail that, if accurate, indicates forced tribute in human beings.

Pompey's eastern campaigns bring Colchis under Roman influence. The region was subsequently administered through local client rulers. The kingdom of Lazica (Egrisi) later emerged as the successor state.

sources.citation: Braund, D. Georgia in Antiquity: A History of Colchis and Transcaucasian Iberia, 550 BC-AD 562 (1994)
Lordkipanidze, O. Phasis: The River and the City in Colchis (2000)
Herodotus, Histories III.97 (c. 440 BCE)
Apollonius Rhodius, Argonautica (3rd century BCE)

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: Original name in Classical Armenian (Grabar). Armenia is one of the oldest continuously named regions in the world — 'Armina' appears in Darius I's Behistun inscription (-520). ETHICS-002: Armenia's history is marked by its position between major empires (Achaemenid, Seleucid, Roman, Parthian, Sasanian) and repeated invasions. The Armenian genocide of 1915-1923, while outside this entity's time scope, is part of a long pattern of violence against Armenians that this database must not obscure. ETHICS-003: Well-attested through Armenian, Greek, Roman, and Persian sources. Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (100, strategy=exact_name, precision=1). CC BY 4.0.

name_variants.name: Kingdom of Armenia
name_variants.context: English name for the ancient Armenian kingdom
name_variants.source: Garsoïan, N. in Cambridge History of Iran III (1983)

name_variants.name: Armenia Magna
name_variants.context: Latin name distinguishing Greater Armenia from Armenia Minor
name_variants.source: Strabo, Geography XI.14

name_variants.name: Armina
name_variants.context: Old Persian name from the Behistun inscription of Darius I
name_variants.source: Darius I, Behistun inscription

name_variants.name: Armenia
name_variants.context: Italian scholarly name
name_variants.source: Garsoïan, N. in Storia di Cambridge dell'Iran (1983)

territory_changes.description: Orontes I (Yervand) establishes the Orontid dynasty as independent rulers following Alexander's death and the breakup of the Achaemenid Empire. The Armenian satrapy under the Achaemenids had maintained significant cultural autonomy.

Tigranes II 'the Great' expands Armenia to its maximum extent, conquering parts of Mesopotamia, Syria, and Cilicia. His new capital Tigranocerta was one of the great Hellenistic cities. This was the largest Armenian state in history.

Partition of Armenia between the Roman and Sasanian Empires (Peace of Acilisene). The eastern (larger) portion went to Sassanid Persia, the western portion to Rome. This partition, deepened in 428 when the Arsacid dynasty was deposed in the east, shaped Armenian history for centuries.

The Sasanian Empire deposes the last Arsacid king, Artaxias IV, and places Armenia under direct marzban (governor) rule. This marks the end of Armenian independent statehood in antiquity, though Armenian identity and the Armenian church (Christianity adopted c. 301 CE) survived and flourished.

sources.citation: Garsoïan, N. in Cambridge History of Iran, Vol. III (1983)
Redgate, A.E. The Armenians (1998)
Moses of Chorene (Movses Khorenatsi), History of the Armenians (5th century CE)
Darius I, Behistun inscription (c. 520 BCE)
Strabo, Geography XI.14 (c. 20 CE)

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: The Chinese name 匈奴 (Xiōngnú) is the only written form, as the Xiongnu had no writing system. The actual pronunciation and self-designation are debated. ETHICS-003: The Xiongnu are known primarily through Chinese sources (Shiji, Hanshu), which are inherently biased as they represent the perspective of a rival civilization. The identification of the Xiongnu with the later Huns who invaded Europe is debated and unresolved. The capital 'Longcheng' (Dragon City) has not been definitively located; coordinates are approximate for the probable region in central Mongolia. Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (-100, strategy=exact_name, precision=1). CC BY 4.0.

name_variants.name: Xiongnu
name_variants.context: Modern Mandarin romanization of the Chinese name
name_variants.source: Di Cosmo, N. Ancient China and Its Enemies (2002)

name_variants.name: Hsiung-nu
name_variants.context: Older Wade-Giles romanization, common in earlier scholarship
name_variants.source: Barfield, T.J. The Perilous Frontier (1989)

name_variants.name: Xiongnu
name_variants.context: Italian scholarly name (same romanization)
name_variants.source: Di Cosmo, N. Ancient China and Its Enemies (2002)

territory_changes.description: Modu Chanyu (Maodun) kills his father and unifies the nomadic tribes of the steppe into a powerful empire. The Shiji provides a detailed narrative. Modu famously trained his warriors to shoot wherever he shot his whistling arrow — including at his own wife and horse — to ensure absolute obedience. The account may be embellished but reflects Chinese fear of Xiongnu military discipline.

Battle of Baideng — Modu besieges Han Emperor Gaozu (Liu Bang), who is forced to accept humiliating heqin ('peace and kinship') treaties, sending Chinese princesses and tribute to the Xiongnu. This reversal of the expected power dynamic deeply shaped Chinese strategic thinking.

Split into Northern and Southern Xiongnu. The Southern Xiongnu submitted to the Han dynasty as a client state. The Northern Xiongnu were eventually defeated and driven westward (89-91 CE). Their subsequent fate — and possible connection to the Huns of European history — remains one of the great unsolved questions of Eurasian history.

sources.citation: Sima Qian, Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian), ch. 110 (c. 90 BCE)
Ban Gu, Hanshu (Book of Han), ch. 94 (c. 80 CE)
Di Cosmo, N. Ancient China and Its Enemies: The Rise of Nomadic Power in East Asian History (2002)
Barfield, T.J. The Perilous Frontier: Nomadic Empires and China (1989)

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: Like the Xiongnu, the Xianbei name is known only in Chinese transcription (鲜卑, Xiānbēi). Their language is classified as Mongolic or para-Mongolic, but this is uncertain. ETHICS-003: The Xianbei confederation was extremely diverse and fragmented. The 'peak' under Tanshihuai was brief. The Xianbei are more significant for what they became — their descendants include the founders of several Chinese dynasties (Northern Wei, Sui, Tang all had Xianbei ancestry). Capital coordinates are approximate for the steppe center, as there was no fixed capital.

name_variants.name: Xianbei
name_variants.context: Modern Mandarin romanization
name_variants.source: Barfield, T.J. The Perilous Frontier (1989)

name_variants.name: Hsien-pi
name_variants.context: Older Wade-Giles romanization
name_variants.source: Gardiner, K.H.J. The Early History of Korea (1969)

name_variants.name: Xianbei
name_variants.context: Italian scholarly name (same romanization)
name_variants.source: Di Cosmo, N. et al. The Cambridge History of Inner Asia (2009)

territory_changes.description: Following the Han-Xiongnu war that shattered the Northern Xiongnu, the Xianbei rapidly expand into the power vacuum on the steppe. The Hou Hanshu (Book of Later Han) records this transition. Many former Xiongnu subjects simply adopted the Xianbei identity.

Under Tanshihuai, the Xianbei briefly unite into a powerful confederation stretching from Manchuria to the Ili Valley. Tanshihuai defeated both the Xiongnu remnants and repulsed Han Chinese attacks. However, the confederation fragmented after his death in 181.

The Xianbei confederation fully fragments into competing groups (Murong, Tuoba, Khitan ancestors, etc.). This date is approximate — the fragmentation was a process, not a single event. These successor groups would profoundly shape Chinese history in subsequent centuries.

sources.citation: Fan Ye, Hou Hanshu (Book of Later Han), ch. 90 (5th century CE compilation of earlier sources)
Barfield, T.J. The Perilous Frontier: Nomadic Empires and China (1989)
Di Cosmo, N., Frank, A.J., & Golden, P.B. (eds.) The Cambridge History of Inner Asia (2009)

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: 'Nabta Playa' is a modern Arabic/English archaeological site name. The original inhabitants' language and self-designation are entirely unknown. ETHICS-003: CRITICAL — Nabta Playa is NOT a 'city-state' in any conventional sense. It was a seasonal ceremonial/pastoral gathering site in the Western Desert of Egypt/Sudan. The entity_type 'city-state' is the closest available schema option but is deeply misleading. The site is significant for some of the earliest known megalithic structures and what may be an astronomical calendar circle, but interpretations are debated. The 'stone circle' may be a simple campsite marker rather than an observatory. Evidence is entirely archaeological with no textual sources of any kind. Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (-5000, strategy=capital_near_centroid, precision=1). CC BY 4.0.

name_variants.name: Nabta Playa
name_variants.context: Modern archaeological site name, from the Arabic name of the playa (dry lake bed)
name_variants.source: Wendorf, F. & Schild, R. Holocene Settlement of the Egyptian Sahara (2001)

name_variants.name: Nabta Playa
name_variants.context: Italian scholarly name (same)
name_variants.source: Wendorf, F. & Schild, R. (2001)

territory_changes.description: Earliest evidence of semi-permanent occupation during the Holocene Wet Phase when the Sahara was relatively verdant. Cattle bones and pottery fragments indicate pastoral communities who gathered seasonally at the playa. There was no 'state' formation — this represents early pastoral community organization.

Peak of ceremonial activity. Construction of megalithic structures, stone alignments, and what Wendorf interprets as a calendar circle. Cattle burials suggest ritual significance. Some scholars (Malville) see astronomical alignments; others are skeptical. The population was likely in the hundreds, not thousands.

Abandonment as the Sahara desiccates (onset of current arid conditions). Populations likely migrated toward the Nile Valley. Some scholars hypothesize a connection between Nabta Playa pastoralists and pre-dynastic Egyptian culture, but this remains speculative.

sources.citation: Wendorf, F. & Schild, R. Holocene Settlement of the Egyptian Sahara, Vol. 1: The Archaeology of Nabta Playa (2001)
Malville, J.M. et al. Megaliths and Neolithic Astronomy in Southern Egypt, Nature 392 (1998)
Applegate, A. et al. Late Neolithic Megalithic Structures at Nabta Playa, in Wendorf & Schild (2001)

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: 'Çatalhöyük' is a modern Turkish name meaning 'forked mound'. The original inhabitants' language is unknown — they predate all known Anatolian languages by millennia. ETHICS-003: Çatalhöyük is one of the most thoroughly excavated Neolithic sites in the world (Mellaart 1960s, Hodder 1993-2018). It was NOT a 'city-state' but a large Neolithic settlement of perhaps 3,000-8,000 people. There is no evidence of social hierarchy, central authority, defensive walls, or administrative structures. Its social organization appears remarkably egalitarian. The entity_type 'city-state' is a schema limitation. Earlier interpretations by Mellaart (a 'Mother Goddess' cult) have been substantially revised by Hodder's work.

name_variants.name: Çatalhöyük
name_variants.context: International scholarly name (Turkish)
name_variants.source: Hodder, I. The Leopard's Tale: Revealing the Mysteries of Çatalhöyük (2006)

name_variants.name: Çatal Hüyük
name_variants.context: Older Turkish spelling used in Mellaart's publications
name_variants.source: Mellaart, J. Çatal Hüyük: A Neolithic Town in Anatolia (1967)

name_variants.name: Çatalhöyük
name_variants.context: Italian scholarly name (same)
name_variants.source: Hodder, I. (2006)

territory_changes.description: Earliest occupation of the East Mound. Settlement grew to cover 13 hectares, making it one of the largest known Neolithic settlements. Houses were tightly packed with roof access and no streets — a unique urban form. Evidence of agriculture (wheat, barley) and animal husbandry (cattle, sheep, goats).

Peak occupation period. Rich symbolic culture including wall paintings, figurines, cattle skulls (bucrania) installed in houses, and elaborate burial practices (bodies interred under house floors). Obsidian trade networks extended to eastern Anatolia and beyond.

Abandonment of the site. Occupation shifted briefly to the smaller West Mound before the location was entirely abandoned. Reasons unknown — possibly soil exhaustion, climate change, or shifting social patterns. There was no violent destruction.

sources.citation: Hodder, I. The Leopard's Tale: Revealing the Mysteries of Çatalhöyük (2006)
Hodder, I. (ed.) Çatalhöyük Excavations: The 2000-2008 Seasons (2013)
Mellaart, J. Çatal Hüyük: A Neolithic Town in Anatolia (1967)
Asouti, E. & Fuller, D. Trees and Woodlands of South India: Archaeological Perspectives (2008)

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: The name 'Dilmun' comes from Sumerian/Akkadian cuneiform texts. The native language of Dilmun's population is unknown. Arabic is used here as the modern language of the region but is anachronistic. ETHICS-003: Dilmun is attested in Mesopotamian texts as a trading partner and a mythological 'paradise' (in the Epic of Gilgamesh and Enki and Ninhursag). The identification with Bahrain is now well-established through archaeological excavation (Qal'at al-Bahrain, a UNESCO World Heritage Site). Dilmun served as a crucial intermediary in the trade between Mesopotamia and the Indus Valley Civilization.

name_variants.name: Dilmun
name_variants.context: English scholarly name from Sumerian/Akkadian
name_variants.source: Potts, D.T. The Arabian Gulf in Antiquity (1990)

name_variants.name: NI.TUK.KI / Tilmun
name_variants.context: Sumerian cuneiform name
name_variants.source: Crawford, H. Dilmun and Its Gulf Neighbours (1998)

name_variants.name: Dilmun
name_variants.context: Italian scholarly name (same)
name_variants.source: Potts, D.T. (1990)

territory_changes.description: Peak of Dilmun's commercial importance as an entrepôt between Mesopotamia and the Indus Valley. Ur III texts record extensive trade with Dilmun. Circular 'Dilmun seals' are found across the Gulf region and in Indus Valley sites, attesting to long-distance trade networks.

Under Neo-Babylonian influence. Inscription of Uperi, king of Dilmun, paying tribute to Sargon II of Assyria (c. -709) shows earlier Assyrian domination. Dilmun's independence was effectively lost long before the formal end.

Absorbed into the Achaemenid Persian Empire following Cyrus the Great's conquest of Babylon. Dilmun ceased to exist as an independent entity, becoming part of the satrapy system. The name survived in later texts but as a geographical rather than political designation.

sources.citation: Potts, D.T. The Arabian Gulf in Antiquity, 2 vols. (1990)
Crawford, H. Dilmun and Its Gulf Neighbours (1998)
Epic of Gilgamesh, Tablet X (c. 2100 BCE original, 7th century BCE Standard Babylonian version)
Qal'at al-Bahrain excavation reports, UNESCO World Heritage nomination dossier (2005)

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: 'Mauretania' is the Latin/Greek name from the ethnic term 'Mauri'. The Berber/Amazigh self-designation for the kingdom is not preserved. The kingdom should not be confused with the modern state of Mauritania, which lies far to the south. ETHICS-003: Mauretania is relatively well-attested through Roman literary sources, coinage, and archaeology. Its last king, Ptolemy of Mauretania (grandson of both Cleopatra VII and Juba II), was murdered by Caligula — an act of arbitrary imperial violence. Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (-100, strategy=exact_name, precision=1). CC BY 4.0.

name_variants.name: Kingdom of Mauretania
name_variants.context: English name for the Berber kingdom in North Africa
name_variants.source: Roller, D.W. The World of Juba II and Kleopatra Selene (2003)

name_variants.name: Mauretania
name_variants.context: Latin name as used by Roman sources
name_variants.source: Pliny the Elder, Natural History V.1

name_variants.name: Mauretania
name_variants.context: Italian scholarly name (same as Latin)
name_variants.source: Roller, D.W. (2003)

territory_changes.description: Earliest attested Mauretanian kings (Baga, who provided troops to the Carthaginians). The kingdom encompassed much of modern Morocco and western Algeria. The Berber/Amazigh population had maintained a distinct identity through Carthaginian and later periods.

Augustus installs Juba II, a Numidian prince educated in Rome and married to Cleopatra Selene (daughter of Cleopatra VII and Mark Antony), as client king. Juba made Iol (renamed Caesarea) a center of Hellenistic learning and art — one of the great cultural capitals of the ancient Mediterranean. The kingdom was nominally independent but thoroughly dependent on Rome.

Emperor Caligula murders King Ptolemy of Mauretania (Juba II's son) during a visit to Rome, allegedly motivated by jealousy of Ptolemy's purple cloak. Suetonius and Dio Cassius record the incident. The subsequent Berber revolt led by Aedemon took years to suppress. The kingdom was divided into two Roman provinces (Mauretania Caesariensis and Mauretania Tingitana).

sources.citation: Roller, D.W. The World of Juba II and Kleopatra Selene: Royal Scholarship on Rome's African Frontier (2003)
Pliny the Elder, Natural History V.1 (c. 77 CE)
Suetonius, Life of Caligula 35 (c. 121 CE)
Dio Cassius, Roman History LIX.25 (c. 220 CE)

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: Tamil name நாகர் (Nākar). The Nagas are mentioned in Sangam literature and later Puranic texts as a distinct people/clan in South India and Sri Lanka. ETHICS-003: CRITICAL — the 'Naga kingdoms' are poorly defined as a political entity. The term 'Naga' (serpent) may refer to diverse groups: tribal peoples, a clan identity, a religious affiliation, or a mythological construct. Sources are overwhelmingly later literary texts, not contemporary inscriptions. The capital coordinates are approximate for the Tamil Nadu region; there was no single Naga capital. Confidence is very low. Separating historical Nagas from mythological Nagas (in Hindu and Buddhist tradition) is extremely difficult. Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (-1, strategy=fuzzy_name, precision=1). CC BY 4.0.

name_variants.name: Naga kingdoms
name_variants.context: English scholarly name for the various Naga polities of South India and Sri Lanka
name_variants.source: Nilakanta Sastri, K.A. A History of South India (1955)

name_variants.name: Nāga
name_variants.context: Sanskrit name used in Puranic literature and Buddhist texts
name_variants.source: Pargiter, F.E. The Purāṇa Text of the Dynasties of the Kali Age (1913)

name_variants.name: Regni Naga
name_variants.context: Italian scholarly translation
name_variants.source: Nilakanta Sastri, K.A. (1955)

territory_changes.description: Naga groups are attested in Sangam poetry (Purananuru, Akananuru) as a distinct people in parts of Tamil Nadu and northern Sri Lanka (Nagadipa). The Mahavamsa mentions a Naga kingdom in Sri Lanka encountered by early Buddhist missionaries. These references likely reflect clan-based polities rather than centralized states.

Naga dynasties attested in the Vidarbha region (modern Maharashtra) through coinage and inscriptions. The Bharsiva/Bharasiva Nagas and later Vakataka-era Nagas left more substantial evidence than the southern Nagas, though the connection between these groups and the earlier Sangam-era Nagas is unclear.

Naga polities are gradually absorbed by expanding Gupta, Pallava, and other larger kingdoms. The Naga identity persists in clan names, place names, and religious symbolism (Naga worship) rather than as a political entity. End date is approximate.

sources.citation: Nilakanta Sastri, K.A. A History of South India from Prehistoric Times to the Fall of Vijayanagar, 4th ed. (1955)
Sangam literature: Purananuru, Akananuru (c. 3rd century BCE - 3rd century CE)
Mahavamsa (Great Chronicle of Sri Lanka, c. 5th century CE compilation)
Pargiter, F.E. The Purāṇa Text of the Dynasties of the Kali Age (1913)

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: The Chinese name 吐谷浑 (Tǔyùhún) represents a Chinese transcription of the founder's name. The Tuyuhun were originally a Xianbei (proto-Mongolic) people who migrated to the Qinghai-Tibetan Plateau and intermarried with local Qiang populations. ETHICS-003: Knowledge comes primarily from Chinese dynastic histories (Jin Shu, Wei Shu, Sui Shu, etc.). The Tuyuhun perspective is not directly preserved. The kingdom occupied a strategically vital position on the Silk Road and between the Chinese, Tibetan, and Turkic spheres of influence.

name_variants.name: Tuyuhun
name_variants.context: Modern Mandarin romanization of the Chinese name
name_variants.source: Molè, G. The T'u-yü-hun from the Northern Wei to the Time of the Five Dynasties (1970)

name_variants.name: T'u-yü-hun
name_variants.context: Older Wade-Giles romanization used in earlier scholarship

name_variants.name: Azha
name_variants.context: Tibetan name for the Tuyuhun
name_variants.source: Beckwith, C.I. The Tibetan Empire in Central Asia (1987)

name_variants.name: Tuyuhun
name_variants.context: Italian scholarly name (same romanization)

territory_changes.description: Murong Tuyuhun, elder son of the Murong Xianbei chieftain, leads his followers westward from Manchuria to the Qinghai-Gansu border region following a succession dispute. The migration covered over 2,000 km. The kingdom that bears his name gradually formed as these Xianbei settlers established dominance over local Qiang and Di populations.

Northern Wei dynasty campaigns against the Tuyuhun, forcing them to temporarily flee westward. The Tuyuhun proved remarkably resilient, repeatedly recovering from military defeats due to their control of difficult terrain (high altitude, arid steppe) inhospitable to lowland Chinese armies.

The Tibetan Empire (Bod/Tubo) conquers the Tuyuhun kingdom, absorbing it into the expanding Tibetan state. The Tuyuhun royal family fled to Tang China. Some Tuyuhun populations were assimilated into the Tibetan population; others maintained a distinct identity for centuries under Tang protection. The Monguor/Tu people of modern Qinghai are sometimes identified as their descendants.

sources.citation: Molè, G. The T'u-yü-hun from the Northern Wei to the Time of the Five Dynasties (1970)
Beckwith, C.I. The Tibetan Empire in Central Asia (1987)
Fang Xuanling et al., Jin Shu (Book of Jin), ch. 97 (648 CE)
Wei Shou, Wei Shu (Book of Wei), ch. 101 (554 CE)
